item.title: Expert System
item.description:
Expert System

An expert system is a live system that combines a knowledge base with an inference engine. The knowledge base holds structured content — accumulated knowledge, state, constraints. The inference engine applies operations to that content — inference rules, normalization, derivation — and produces output. Together they constitute a system that reasons: it takes accumulated knowledge and derives conclusions from it.

The term comes from classical AI (1970s–1990s), where an expert system consisted of a knowledge base encoding domain expertise and an inference engine that applied rules to answer queries. This pattern strips the domain-specific connotations and makes it available as a structural concept.

item.title: Anthropic
item.description:
Anthropic is a Public Benefit Corporation founded in 2021 by Dario Amodei (CEO), Daniela Amodei (President), and several former OpenAI researchers. Its corporate purpose is “the responsible development and maintenance of advanced AI for the long-term benefit of humanity.” It is headquartered in San Francisco.

Anthropic builds and operates the Claude family of large language models. Its principal alignment technique is Constitutional AI (CAI): instead of relying solely on human feedback to shape model behavior, CAI provides the model with an explicit set of principles — a “constitution” — against which it evaluates and revises its own outputs. The constitution draws from the UN Declaration of Human Rights, trust and safety best practices, principles from other AI labs, and efforts to incorporate non-Western perspectives. The technique was published in 2022 (Bai et al.) and remains the foundation of Claude’s training process.

item.title: Claude
item.description:
Claude is Anthropic’s family of large language models, built on the transformer architecture and trained using Constitutional AI and RLHF. Claude powers the consumer interface at claude.ai, the developer API, and Claude Code.

Each Claude generation ships in multiple tiers that trade capability for speed and cost:

  • Opus — highest capability, deepest reasoning, best for complex analysis and agentic tasks
  • Sonnet — balanced performance and cost, the default for most applications
  • Haiku — fastest and cheapest, optimized for high-throughput tasks

The current generation (as of early 2026) comprises Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, and Haiku 4.5.

item.title: Context Engineering
item.description: Context engineering is the discipline of designing the complete information environment a language model receives — system prompts, retrieved documents, tool definitions, conversation history, and instruction files — to maximize task performance within the context window budget. The term became established in June 2025 when Shopify CEO Tobi Lutke and former OpenAI researcher Andrej Karpathy publicly endorsed it. Lutke described it as “the art of providing all the context for the task to be plausibly solvable by the LLM.” Gartner defines it as “designing and structuring the relevant data, workflows and environment so AI systems can understand intent, make better decisions and deliver contextual, enterprise-aligned outcomes.”

item.title: Context Window
item.description:
The context window is the maximum number of tokens a large language model can process in a single interaction. Everything the model reads and generates — the system prompt, the user’s message, the conversation history, tool definitions, tool results, and the model’s own output — must fit within this fixed budget. Once the context window is full, earlier content must be dropped or compressed to make room for new information.

The constraint is architectural. The transformer’s self-attention mechanism computes pairwise attention scores between all tokens, requiring O(N²) compute and O(N) memory for a sequence of N tokens. Extending the context window requires proportionally more resources per forward pass. Research on efficient attention (sparse attention, sliding window, linear approximations) reduces the constant factor but does not eliminate the fundamental scaling relationship.

item.title: Embedding
item.description:
An embedding is a fixed-length vector of floating-point numbers that represents a piece of text (or image, or audio) in a continuous space where semantic similarity corresponds to geometric proximity. Two sentences about the same topic produce vectors that are close together (high cosine similarity); two sentences about unrelated topics produce vectors that are far apart.

Mikolov et al. introduced the foundational word embedding models (Word2Vec) in “Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space” (2013), demonstrating that neural networks trained on word co-occurrence patterns produce vector spaces with algebraic structure — the famous example: vector(“king”) - vector(“man”) + vector(“woman”) ≈ vector(“queen”).

item.title: Fine-Tuning
item.description:
Fine-tuning is the process of continuing the training of a pre-trained neural network on a smaller, task-specific dataset to adapt its behavior for a particular use case. The pre-trained model has already learned general patterns from a large corpus; fine-tuning adjusts its weights to specialize in a narrower domain or capability while retaining its general knowledge.

The concept predates large language models. Transfer learning — training on a large dataset then specializing on a small one — was established in computer vision (Yosinski et al., 2014) before being applied to NLP. For language models, the two-stage pipeline (pre-train on large text, fine-tune on task-specific data) was popularized by Howard and Ruder (2018, ULMFiT) and refined by the GPT and BERT model families.

item.title: GraphRAG
item.description:
GraphRAG is a retrieval-augmented generation pattern that retrieves from a knowledge graph rather than a flat vector store. Standard RAG embeds document chunks as vectors and retrieves by semantic similarity. GraphRAG extracts entities and relationships from documents, builds a graph connecting them, and traverses that graph to answer queries that require reasoning across multiple documents or multiple hops of relationship.

Microsoft Research introduced the term and architecture in “From Local to Global: A Graph RAG Approach to Query-Focused Summarization” (Edge et al., 2024). The core insight: standard RAG retrieves passages similar to the query, but many questions require synthesizing information scattered across passages that are not individually similar to the query. “What are the main themes in this dataset?” requires global understanding. “How does X relate to Y through Z?” requires following a chain of relationships. Standard RAG retrieves individual chunks; GraphRAG retrieves subgraphs.

item.title: Hallucination
item.description: A hallucination is text generated by a large language model that is fluent and plausible but factually false. The model produces false statements with the same confidence as true ones because its generation mechanism — predicting the next token based on statistical patterns — optimizes for coherence, not truth. A model trained on text about historical events generates text that reads like history but may contain invented dates, fabricated quotes, or nonexistent sources.

item.title: Neural Network
item.description: A neural network is a computational model composed of layers of interconnected nodes (artificial neurons) that learn to approximate functions by adjusting the strengths (weights) of connections between nodes during training. Each node takes weighted inputs, applies a nonlinear activation function, and passes its output to nodes in the next layer. The network learns by comparing its output to a desired output, computing the error, and propagating that error backward through the layers to adjust weights — a process called backpropagation (Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams, 1986).

item.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
item.description:
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a pattern where a large language model retrieves relevant documents from an external knowledge base before generating a response. The retrieved documents are injected into the context window alongside the user’s query, grounding the model’s generation in specific source material. Lewis et al. introduced the term and architecture in “Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks” (NeurIPS 2020).

The standard RAG pipeline has three stages:

  1. Indexing. Documents are split into chunks, each chunk is converted into a vector embedding, and the embeddings are stored in a vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, pgvector, or similar).

item.title: RLHF
item.description: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is a training method in which human evaluators compare pairs of model outputs, a reward model learns to predict which output humans prefer, and the language model is then optimized to produce outputs that score highly under the reward model. The method was described by Christiano et al. (2017, “Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences”) and applied to language models at scale by Ouyang et al. (2022, “Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback”) at OpenAI, producing InstructGPT.

item.title: System Prompt
item.description:
A system prompt is a block of text provided to a large language model at the start of an interaction that establishes the model’s role, behavioral constraints, available tools, output format, and any other instructions the deployer wants the model to follow. It is distinct from the user’s message: the user provides the task; the system prompt provides the context in which the task is interpreted.

In API terms, the system prompt occupies a dedicated system role in the message array (or equivalent, depending on the provider). The model treats system prompt content as high-priority instructions that frame all subsequent interaction. The system prompt is typically invisible to the end user but is visible to the model — it appears at the beginning of the context window and consumes tokens from the same budget as the conversation.

item.title: Token
item.description:
A token is the atomic unit of text that a large language model processes. Models do not operate on characters or words directly; they operate on tokens — subword fragments produced by a tokenizer that splits text into pieces from a fixed vocabulary. The word “unbelievable” might be tokenized as [“un”, “believ”, “able”]; common words like “the” are single tokens; rare words or code identifiers are split into multiple tokens.

Tokenizers are trained on large text corpora to find a vocabulary (typically 32,000–100,000 tokens) that minimizes the total number of tokens needed to represent the training data. The dominant algorithm is Byte Pair Encoding (BPE), introduced for neural machine translation by Sennrich, Haddow, and Birch (2016). BPE starts with individual characters and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs until the vocabulary reaches the target size. The result is a vocabulary where common words and subwords are single tokens and rare sequences are composed from smaller pieces.

item.title: Transformer
item.description: The transformer is a neural network architecture introduced by Vaswani et al. in “Attention Is All You Need” (2017). It processes input sequences using self-attention — a mechanism that allows every element in a sequence to attend to every other element, computing relevance weights dynamically rather than relying on fixed-distance connections. This replaced the recurrent architectures (LSTMs, GRUs) that previously dominated sequence modeling, which processed inputs one step at a time and struggled with long-range dependencies.
